title: What If? Introducing a New Podcast on Digital in Culture
content:
What if the next exhibition you visit was shaped by an algorithm? What if your museum’s archive could speak forty languages by tomorrow morning? What if the next “revolutionary” tool turns out to be a fad — and the real shift happened somewhere quieter, five years ago?

These are the kinds of questions we want to sit with. Not to answer them neatly, but to open them up.

Today, we are launching What If? — a new podcast series exploring the emerging pathways of digital technology in the cultural sector.

Why this podcast, why now

At our Forum last November, one thing became clear: the cultural sector doesn’t need more hype about digital technology. It needs better conversations about it , ones that hold both the promise and the friction in the same breath.

That’s the premise of What If? Each episode brings together two voices, one from the digital world, one from the cultural world — and asks them to think out loud together. Not a pitch, not a panel. A real conversation about what’s actually changing, what it opens up, and what it demands of us in return.

Every episode follows the same arc:

  • Discover — what’s actually happening right now: a tool, a shift, a case worth knowing

title: Meet our 4 New Members!
content:
Last June, we just got a little bigger, and a lot more diverse. We’re thrilled to welcome four new members to our network! Different countries, different disciplines, different scales, but all working, in their own way, at the intersection of culture, technology, and people.

Here’s a closer look at who they are.

Laboratorio Lapsus is a non-profit organisation working at the crossroads of historical research, participatory history, education and community engagement. Its mission: making history a living, shared practice rather than something told to communities. Lapsus builds public history and educational projects that make people the active protagonists of their own history.

Their work spans oral histories of marginalised and underrepresented communities, critical nodes of contemporary history, and public narratives, always approached with historiographical rigour, but through collaborative, participatory and creative methods. Lapsus coordinates and takes part in European projects, weaving transnational partnerships across the cultural and third sector, with the aim of fostering critical thinking, civic engagement, and a deeper sense of belonging to shared cultural heritage.
